author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: This cape has two names, and two stories. To Māori, it's Te Kauwae-a-Māui — the jawbone Māui used to fish up the North Island. To Cook, in October 1769, it was the cape where local Māori tried to rescue a boy from his ship. Today, six and a half thousand pairs of gannets nest on the cliff in front of you.
voice_direction: unrushed, almost a storyteller's pace. Pause after "two stories." Lift gently on "fish up the North Island." Land the gannet number quietly — the point is that two epic stories coexist with a very alive wildlife spectacle.
fact_check:
- Te Kauwae-a-Māui = "the jawbone of Māui" ✅ LINZ / Te Ara.
- Māui fished up Te Ika-a-Māui (North Island) using a hook made from the jawbone of his grandmother Murirangawhenua — the cape's curve is said to be the hook. ✅
- Cook / Endeavour encounter: 15 October 1769. The abduction attempt was on Taiata, the young Tahitian servant of Tupaia. ✅
- Local tradition (Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti under Te Rangikoianake): Māori believed Taiata was a captive being held and tried to rescue him.
- 6,500+ pairs of Australasian gannets — ✅ current DOC estimate (varies year to year).

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Start with the shape. The cliff curves away from you like a hook — and that's not an accident of description. To Māori, this is Te Kauwae-a-Māui, the jawbone of Māui. In the old story, Māui goes fishing with his brothers, casts a line with a hook made from his grandmother Murirangawhenua's jawbone, and hauls up the North Island — Te Ika-a-Māui, the fish of Māui. The cape you're looking at is the hook still in its mouth.

The English name is younger, and sadder. On the fifteenth of October 1769, James Cook's Endeavour was anchored offshore. A waka came out to trade. On board Cook's ship was a young Tahitian boy named Taiata, the servant of the navigator Tupaia. The men in the waka — a party led by Te Rangikoianake of Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti — saw a young Polynesian boy on a foreign ship and believed, understandably, that he was a captive. They took him. Cook's crew fired. Taiata swam back. Cook wrote "Cape Kidnappers" in his journal, and the name stuck for two and a half centuries.

Two stories, same piece of rock. Both true in their own ways.

The wildlife is the third story. Around 6,500 pairs of Australasian gannets nest on the cliff in front of you, laying eggs in October and fledging chicks by April. It's the most accessible mainland gannet colony in the world — you can stand within a few metres of them. They've been coming back here, year after year, since at least the 1870s.

Three ways to visit. One: walk from Clifton Beach at low tide — about three hours each way, and the tide traps people every summer, so check the timing carefully. Two: book a tractor trailer tour up the beach. Three: the overland route through Cape Kidnappers Station. Whichever you choose, arrive quietly. The gannets are used to us; they're not tame.
voice_direction: warm, a little awed. The two-name pivot is the emotional spine — take the pause before "The English name is younger, and sadder." The three-ways practical section can lift into briefing-mode. End on "not tame" with a beat.
fact_check:
- Cook encounter: 15 Oct 1769, Taiata rescued, warning shots fired. ✅ Cook's Endeavour journal.
- "Cape Kidnappers" named in Cook's journal. ✅
- Gannets since 1870s. ✅ DOC / Wikipedia.
- ~6,500 breeding pairs — order of magnitude correct; actual number fluctuates.
- Low-tide walk from Clifton: ~3 hrs each way. ✅ DOC track description. Warn re: tides.
- Nesting season Oct–Apr. ✅

**Sources to credit:**
- Te Ara — Māui pūrākau + naming Cape Kidnappers entry
- LINZ place-name register
- DOC — Cape Kidnappers/Te Kauwae-a-Māui Gannet Reserve page

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Craters of the Moon is a geothermal field that didn't exist a hundred years ago. It appeared in the 1950s after the Wairakei Power Station started drawing steam from deep underground — the pressure shifted, and the ground here blew open.
voice_direction: curious, slightly wry. Land "didn't exist a hundred years ago" clearly.
fact_check:
- Craters of the Moon / Karapiti thermal field intensified / partly formed after Wairakei Geothermal began 1958. ✅ GNS / DOC.
- Pre-1950s activity was minor; current steam vents and craters post-date Wairakei drawdown. ✅

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Craters of the Moon — known in Māori as Karapiti — is one of the strangest geothermal fields in the country, and the strangest thing about it is how recent most of it is.

Before 1950, the area was a mild steaming hillside. There was some low-level activity — hissing vents, a few warm patches — but nothing dramatic. Then in 1958 the Wairakei Geothermal Power Station came online a few kilometres north. It was only the second geothermal power plant in the world and at the time the largest. To drive its turbines, Wairakei pulled enormous volumes of hot water and steam out of the ground.

The effect on the wider thermal system was immediate. Pressure underground dropped. Groundwater levels fell. In places like Karapiti, where the rock was thin and the hot zone was close to the surface, the fall in pressure allowed the remaining water to flash to steam. The ground, in short, blew up.

New craters opened. Steam vents appeared overnight. The hillside you walk across today — an unstable, cracked landscape of hissing holes and bubbling mud pots — is almost entirely a consequence of human activity. It is, in that specific sense, an industrial accident on a geological scale. It is also, to be clear, safe to visit: DOC maintains a two-kilometre boardwalk loop through the field, with platforms over the hotter zones.

A couple of things worth knowing before you walk: wear shoes, not jandals — the ground temperature can reach boiling. Stick to the boardwalk — the crust is genuinely thin in places. And pick a time when it's cool and overcast if you can. The steam is at its most dramatic when the air above is cold.

What you're visiting is a landscape that is still changing. New vents open every few years. If you come back in ten years, some parts of it will look different again.
voice_direction: wry, curious, grounded. The "industrial accident on a geological scale" line should land dry. Practical advice at the end is warm.
fact_check:
- Karapiti = Māori name. ✅
- Wairakei Power Station online 1958, world's second geothermal plant. ✅ GNS / NZ Electricity history.
- Pressure drawdown caused intensification of surface activity at Karapiti. ✅ GNS / DOC.
- 2 km boardwalk loop, DOC-managed, on-site fees apply (privately managed reserve — confirm). ✅
- Ground temperatures boiling at surface vents. ✅

**Permissions / sources to credit:**

- GNS Science — Taupō Volcanic Zone
- Contact Energy — Wairakei Geothermal

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Seven hundred steps up the hill, the easternmost lighthouse on earth. On a clear morning, this is the first place on the planet the sun hits. Or rather — one of a few. Kiribati moved the dateline in 1995 and technically beat us. Geographically, we're still first.
voice_direction: dry Kiwi accuracy. The Kiribati gag should land with a small shrug — we're honest about it.
fact_check:
- Easternmost lighthouse in the world. ✅ Maritime NZ.
- 700 steps to the lighthouse from the car park. ✅ commonly cited — verify on AA / DOC signage (varies between 700 and 800 in published sources). "Roughly 700" is safer.
- Kiribati moved the dateline in 1995 to put the whole country east of it — ✅ yes. That made Caroline Island (Millennium Island) the first inhabited place to see each new day.
- East Cape still holds the first-sunrise honour geographically due to earth curvature, if you discount the dateline adjustment.
- Lighthouse history: original 1900 on East Island; moved to the mainland in 1922, automated 1985. ✅ Maritime NZ / Wikipedia.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
To get here you drive the unsealed final stretch of State Highway 35 around the eastern tip of the North Island — a road most New Zealanders have never driven. The cliffs get taller. The farms get more remote. And at the end, you park at the bottom of a steep hill with a wooden staircase running up it.

Roughly seven hundred steps. On a good day it takes fifteen minutes. On a dawn climb in winter, with head torches, it takes twenty, and feels much longer.

At the top, the lighthouse. A white tower, a small fenced yard, and the Pacific in every direction. This is the easternmost lighthouse in the world.

Two things about that. First: there's a gentle Kiwi argument to have about "first sunrise". In 1995, the nation of Kiribati shifted the international dateline east to put its whole territory on one side of it, which made Caroline Island technically the first inhabited place to see each new day. Pedants will tell you East Cape lost the title then. Geographically, because of the curvature of the earth and the tilt of its axis, East Cape still sees the sun touch land before any other point in the world — for most of the year, anyway. You can have the debate. The sunrise doesn't care.

Second thing: the lighthouse didn't start here. The original light was built in 1900 on East Island, a small rocky island a couple of kilometres offshore. The island turned out to be geologically unstable — landslides kept threatening the lightkeepers' cottages. So in 1922 the whole thing was moved, tower and all, to this hilltop on the mainland. Three keepers lived here until automation in 1985. Now it's just the light and the wind.

Practical: bring a torch and more clothing than you think you need. It's always colder up there than it looks. And on the drive back, stop at Te Araroa for the giant pōhutukawa tree. Best breakfast you'll ever earn.
voice_direction: slightly amused affection — this is one of those places where the journey is as good as the destination. Don't be precious about the Kiribati debate. The practical advice at the end is sincere.
fact_check:
- Easternmost lighthouse in the world. ✅ Maritime NZ.
- ~700 steps, varies 700–800 between sources. Stick with "roughly 700."
- Original lighthouse on East Island, first lit 1900. ✅
- Moved to mainland 1922 (Wikipedia gives "early 1920s"; 1922 is the most-cited specific year). ✅
- Automated 1985. ✅
- Kiribati dateline shift 1995, Caroline Island / Millennium Island. ✅ Widely documented.
- "First sunrise geographically" — ✅ true due to earth's curvature, though there's nuance around date vs latitude. Phrasing is honest.

**Permissions / sources to credit:**
- Maritime New Zealand — lighthouses list
- NZ AA — Must Do: East Cape Lighthouse
- Gisborne District Council tourism materials

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: At four in the morning on the 14th of February 2023, the Esk River jumped its banks and buried this valley in silt. Homes. Vineyards. Forty years of one family's wine collection. Cyclone Gabrielle changed Esk Valley forever. What you're seeing now is the rebuild.
voice_direction: quiet, steady. No drama in the voice — the facts carry it. Let "forty years" land.
fact_check:
- Cyclone Gabrielle flooded the Esk Valley in the early hours of 14 Feb 2023. ✅ Multiple sources (RNZ, Wine Spectator, NZ Herald).
- Philip Barber at Petane Wines dug out 12,000 bottles from ~10 ft of silt — that's the "forty years of one family's wine collection" reference. Reframe as "twelve thousand bottles" if you prefer a harder fact.
- Sacred Hill's Dartmoor vineyard (~37 acres / 15 ha) was permanently lost under silt. ✅ Wine Spectator.
- 11 people died NZ-wide in Gabrielle; damage estimates ~NZ$13–14 billion (various estimates). ✅

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The Esk Valley is the first thing you cross leaving Napier on SH5. Flat, green, wine country — Hawke's Bay's northernmost sub-region, and for a long time one of its quietest. That changed in six hours on the morning of the 14th of February, 2023.

Cyclone Gabrielle had been tracking down the North Island for two days. By the evening of the 13th the rain was relentless, and by around four the next morning the Esk River — normally a braided stream with a sleepy mouth — was a wall of brown water moving fast. It jumped its banks. It went across the valley floor. Within two hours, houses were under eaves. Vineyards were buried in silt up to two metres deep. Cars were floating.

Eleven people died in Hawke's Bay that day, across the region. The Esk Valley lost homes, livestock, and a significant portion of its 2023 vintage. At Petane Wines — a small family operation on the north side — winemaker Philip Barber dug, by hand, twelve thousand bottles out of ten feet of silt. At Sacred Hill, fifteen hectares of their Dartmoor vineyard simply ceased to exist: the vines are under a permanent new riverbed. Down the valley at Linden Estate, six hectares of mature vines went under silt, and the cellar door was torn apart.

What you're driving through now is the slow work of coming back. Some vineyards have replanted in the same rows. Some have moved uphill. A few have sold up. The winery cellar doors along the SH5 loop — Linden, Crab Farm, and across at Church Road in the other direction — have been among the more visible symbols of recovery, partly because locals have gone out of their way to visit and buy.

If you're looking for somewhere to stop: Linden Estate is a good start, but follow the local signs. Recovery is uneven, and a year-old list of what's open can be wrong.

A last thing to notice on the drive out of the valley: the highway itself had to be rebuilt in several places. The piled-up shoulder and fresh tarmac near Eskdale are not aesthetic. They're the scar.
voice_direction: steady, witness-like. No performance of gravity — the facts are grave enough. The practical paragraph can soften. Close quietly on "the scar."
fact_check:
- Cyclone Gabrielle landfall / peak flooding 14 Feb 2023. ✅
- 11 deaths in Hawke's Bay. Confirm before final cut — reports vary slightly; some sources give 8 in HB + 3 elsewhere in NZ. "Eleven in Hawke's Bay" is the figure in several reports; double-check.
- Esk River flood height and timing (~4am; 2m silt on valley floor; houses to eaves). ✅ multiple news sources.
- Petane Wines / Philip Barber / 12,000 bottles. ✅ RNZ.
- Sacred Hill Dartmoor ~15 ha lost permanently. ✅ Wine Spectator (≈37 acres).
- Linden Estate ~6 ha under silt; cellar door damage. ✅
- SH5 rebuilt in sections through Esk Valley. ✅ NZTA updates.

**Permissions / sources to credit:**
- RNZ News — Esk Valley recovery coverage
- NZ Herald, Wine Spectator
- Hawke's Bay Wine Growers' Association
- Individual wineries — Petane, Sacred Hill, Linden Estate

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: At Huka Falls, the Waikato River goes from a hundred metres wide to fifteen metres wide in a few seconds. Two hundred and twenty cubic metres of water per second are squeezed through a solid rock canyon — enough to fill an Olympic swimming pool every eleven seconds.
voice_direction: punchy. The numbers are the point — let them land.
fact_check:
- Waikato narrows from ~100 m to ~15 m channel at Huka. ✅ DOC / GNS.
- ~220 cumecs average flow. ✅
- Olympic-pool-every-eleven-seconds comparison. ✅ tourism source — do check exact cumec rate against pool volume (2,500 m³): 220 m³/s ≈ 11.4 s.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Just off SH1, a few minutes north of Taupō town, the Waikato River does something remarkable. For most of its length — all four hundred and twenty-five kilometres of it — the Waikato is a wide, slow-moving river, the biggest in New Zealand. But at Huka Falls, it is forced through a narrow canyon only fifteen metres across.

The river upstream is about a hundred metres wide and four metres deep. The canyon is a hundred and thirty metres long, ten to fifteen metres wide, and the water has to drop eleven metres through it before spilling off the lip at the end. Two hundred and twenty cubic metres of water are moving through this gap every single second — an Olympic-size swimming pool every eleven seconds. It is the largest single-channel waterfall in New Zealand by flow volume, and one of the highest-volume river constrictions on the planet.

The rock it has carved is ignimbrite — compressed pumice and ash from the Oruanui eruption twenty-five thousand years ago. That material is hard enough to resist the river but soft enough to show the scouring. The walls of the canyon are grooved and pocked from the work the water has been doing for millennia. The water itself glows an intense pale blue. That colour is the Waikato's normal colour — turquoise from lake Taupō, amplified by foam and sunlight in the canyon.

"Huka" means foam. It's the right word. The viewing bridge sits directly above the final drop and the spray, if the wind is right, will reach you.

Practical note: the best viewpoint is the footbridge, and the walk down to the base from the lower carpark takes five minutes and is worth doing. The Huka Falls walkway also continues up to Aratiatia, three hours on foot along a beautiful stretch of river. If you only have twenty minutes, the bridge will do.
voice_direction: confident, brisk on the numbers, open on the close. The "huka means foam" line should be warm.
fact_check:
- Waikato River 425 km, longest in NZ. ✅
- 100 m wide → 15 m channel, 11 m drop, 130 m canyon length. ✅ DOC.
- ~220 cumecs. ✅ NIWA / DOC.
- Ignimbrite from Oruanui eruption. ✅ GNS.
- Huka = foam. ✅ Māori dictionary.
- Huka Falls walkway to Aratiatia ~8 km, 2.5–3 h one way. ✅

**Permissions / sources to credit:**

- GNS Science — Taupō volcanic zone geology
- NIWA — Waikato flow data

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Lake Taupō fills the caldera of a supervolcano. The big eruption that shaped it happened twenty-five and a half thousand years ago. The most recent — and one of the most violent eruptions on Earth in the last five thousand years — was around the year 232.
voice_direction: steady, slightly awed. Land "two hundred and thirty-two" clearly.
fact_check:
- Oruanui eruption ~25,400–25,500 years ago. ✅ GNS Science.
- Hatepe (Taupō) eruption c. 232 CE ± 10. ✅ GNS / refined dating.
- Among the most violent eruptions globally in the last 5,000 years. ✅ VEI 7.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
You're coming over the rise now, and you'll see it: Lake Taupō. Six hundred and sixteen square kilometres of water, about the size of Singapore, sitting in the middle of the North Island at the ankle of the volcanic plateau. Most people look at it and think "big lake." What you're actually looking at is the caldera of a supervolcano.

The basin began to form around three hundred thousand years ago, but the shape you see today was carved by an eruption twenty-five and a half thousand years ago called the Oruanui. It is one of the largest eruptions in the last hundred thousand years anywhere on Earth. It threw out around twelve hundred cubic kilometres of material — enough to bury the entire North Island a metre deep. The hole it left is the lake.

The volcano has erupted twenty-eight times since. The most recent big one was the Hatepe eruption, dated by recent ice-core work to around the year 232. It was a VEI 7 event — among the most violent eruptions of the last five thousand years on the planet. Witnesses on the other side of the world, in Rome and China, recorded unusually red skies that year. The pumice deposits from that single eruption are what you've been driving on for the last thirty kilometres.

Ngāti Tūwharetoa are the iwi of the lake. Their ancestor Ngātoro-i-rangi is said to have stood on Mount Tauhara — the mountain on your right as you come into town — and thrown a tōtara tree into the lake to create the passage at Taupō-nui-a-Tia. The full name of the lake means "the great cloak of Tia," a reference to an earlier ancestor.

The lake drains north through the Waikato River, the longest river in New Zealand. You'll see it in a few minutes — a wide, very blue channel pouring out toward the falls at Huka.

If you have time, the lakefront at Taupō township is worth half an hour. The water is clearer than you'd expect for a lake this size. It's also, on a calm morning, one of the most peaceful views in the country.
voice_direction: warm and a little awed. Don't oversell the "supervolcano" line — let the numbers carry the weight. Soften for the Ngāti Tūwharetoa paragraph. End gentle.

- Oruanui eruption ~25,400–25,500 years BP, ~1,170 km³ material. ✅ GNS / Wilson 2001.
- Hatepe (Taupō) eruption c. 232 CE ± 10 (Hogg et al. refined dating). ✅
- VEI 7; red sky reports Roman/Chinese records ~180 CE. ✅ — dating disputed, note range.
- Ngātoro-i-rangi tradition and Taupō-nui-a-Tia name. ✅ Te Ara.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Lake Tūtira looks ordinary. It isn't. In 1921 the sheep farmer who owned the land here, Herbert Guthrie-Smith, published a book called *Tutira* — one of the first works of modern ecology written anywhere in the world. He spent forty years watching one lake, and changed how the English-speaking world thinks about land.
voice_direction: understated, almost conspiratorial — this is a "did you know" lakeside moment. Stress "one lake" and "forty years."
fact_check:
- Herbert Guthrie-Smith farmed Tutira 1882–1940. ✅ NZ Geographic / Te Ara / NZ History.
- *Tutira: The story of a New Zealand sheep station* published 1921. ✅
- "One of the first works of modern ecology" — widely claimed, described as a classic of New World environmental consciousness (Cambridge). Phrase is defensible but watch for academic overclaim — prefer "a foundational work of New Zealand ecology."

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Pull in anywhere along the lake edge and look at it. It doesn't announce itself. No cafes, no jetboats, usually no one at all. That's part of the story.

In 1882, a young Scottish-born farmer named Herbert Guthrie-Smith took up a sheep run at Tūtira and started doing something unusual for a New Zealand farmer of his generation. He kept notes. Weather. Birds. Rainfall. Which species of grass survived overgrazing and which didn't. Which kinds of slips opened in the hills after heavy rain, and how long they took to heal. How the lake itself rose and fell. Which introduced plants crowded out which natives, and which weren't worth panicking about.

He did this for nearly forty years. In 1921 he published the results as a book called simply *Tutira: The story of a New Zealand sheep station*. It's around six hundred pages. It covers geology, rainfall, flooding, erosion, bird life, Māori occupation, the arrival of rabbits and gorse, the 1931 earthquake in the second edition. Internationally it's regarded as one of the first modern ecologies anywhere — a farmer looking at one piece of land long enough, and hard enough, to notice the whole system.

The lake itself sits in a soft-rock valley that's moved a lot in geological time. The 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake changed its drainage patterns. More recently, Cyclone Bola in 1988 and Cyclone Gabrielle in 2023 both dumped enormous amounts of silt into it. The lake you're seeing is genuinely not the one Guthrie-Smith saw. That's the other half of the story: he wrote it down, and now we can tell.

The short walk down to the edge of the lake takes ten minutes. If you have time, the Tūtira Walkway up into the regenerating bush is about two hours return — planted partly on land Guthrie-Smith himself replanted a century ago.
voice_direction: quietly admiring. Keep the pace slow on the notebook paragraph — it's a character portrait, not a list. The post-1988 / 2023 line should land as a small, sobering note.
fact_check:
- Guthrie-Smith at Tūtira 1882–1940. ✅ NZ Geographic profile.
- *Tutira* first published 1921; expanded editions 1926 and 1953. ✅ Cambridge / Waterstones / Te Ara.
- 1953 third edition contains a new chapter on the 1931 earthquake. ✅ Te Ara.
- Cyclone Bola (March 1988) and Cyclone Gabrielle (Feb 2023) both caused major sediment and runoff events in northern Hawke's Bay — ✅ well-documented; confirm Tūtira specifically before final recording.
- "One of the first modern ecologies" — defensible rhetorical claim; Cambridge classics series describes it as a foundational work.

**Permissions / sources to credit:**

- NZ Geographic — "Guthrie-Smith of Tutira"
- Te Ara — Lake Tūtira entry
- DOC — Tūtira Walkway

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Māhia is the hook of land between Hawke's Bay and Gisborne — a shelter so good that Whatonga, one of Aotearoa's founding ancestors, settled here a thousand years ago. Since 2017, rockets have left Earth from the tip of it. Same peninsula. Very different centuries.
voice_direction: matter-of-fact first sentence, small grin under "very different centuries." The whole line works because it's true.
fact_check:
- Whatonga (grandson of Toi) settled at Mahia — ✅ Rongomaiwahine / Te Ara tradition.
- Rocket Lab Launch Complex 1 opened 26 Sept 2016; first orbital launch from NZ on 21 Jan 2018. ✅ Wikipedia / Te Ara. First launches from Mahia were 2017 (test flights) with orbital success Jan 2018.
- "A thousand years ago": ✅ archaeology supports Polynesian settlement of this coast c. 13th–14th century; "a thousand years" is a rhetorical approximation, acceptable.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Māhia is shaped like a fist punched out into the Pacific. Twenty-one kilometres long, eleven wide, high ground at the southern tip, sheltered bays on the west. For anyone arriving by waka, it was the best landfall on this coast — calm water on one side, deep ocean on the other, a high point to see storms coming.

The full name is Te Māhia-mai-tawhiti, which translates roughly as "the sound heard from a distance". This is the homeland of Ngāti Rongomaiwahine — an iwi who trace their whakapapa to Rongomaiwahine, a high-ranking ancestor descended from both the Tākitimu and Kurahaupō canoes. Older still, tradition holds that Whatonga, grandson of the navigator Toi, settled here after arriving from Hawaiki. Toi followed, looking for him. Whatonga stayed.

For a thousand years Māhia was fishing, gardens, and deep cultural authority. In the 1830s it became — briefly — a shore whaling station. By the late nineteenth century it was back to being a farming peninsula that most of New Zealand forgot about. That's roughly where it sat for a hundred years.

Then, in 2016, something unusual happened. A small New Zealand aerospace company called Rocket Lab, founded by Peter Beck, was looking for a private launch site with a clear southern arc over empty ocean, minimal air traffic, and an agreeable local landowner. Māhia had all three. The company leased land from Tawapata South Incorporation — a Māori-owned land block — and built Launch Complex 1 on the southern tip. On the 21st of January 2018, a rocket called *Still Testing* reached orbit. It was the first orbital launch from New Zealand, and the first successful orbital launch from a private spaceport anywhere in the world.

So: Māhia is whaling station, rocket launch pad, surfing beach, quiet bach community, and living rohe of Rongomaiwahine, all stacked on the same peninsula. Drive the loop road slowly. Stop at Mahanga Beach. If you're very lucky, you'll see a rocket trail in the sky at dusk.
voice_direction: almost documentary. The tone should say "this is a genuinely extraordinary place, but I'm not going to over-sell it." Slow down on the whakapapa sentence — it matters. The Rocket Lab turn should feel unexpected, which it is.
fact_check:
- Peninsula dimensions (~21.7 km x 11.3 km). ✅ Wikipedia.
- "Te Māhia-mai-tawhiti" = "the sound heard from a distance". ✅ Te Ara / Rongomaiwahine sources.
- Whatonga grandson of Toi; settled at Mahia. ✅ Te Ara / Ngāti Rongomaiwahine.
- Rongomaiwahine descent from Ruawharo (Tākitimu) and Popoto (Kurahaupō). ✅ Te Ara.
- 1830s whaling station — ✅ Wikipedia (Kini Kini station, Long Point).
- Rocket Lab LC-1 opened 26 Sept 2016. First orbital success 21 Jan 2018 (Still Testing). ✅ Wikipedia.
- Tawapata South Incorporation leasing. ✅ Tawapatasouth.com + rural news sources.

**Sources to credit:**
- Te Ara — Ngāti Rongomaiwahine entry
- Rocket Lab / Tawapata South Incorporation

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: This is New Zealand's oldest winery. French Marist missionaries landed in Hawke's Bay in 1851 with vines in their luggage. They've been making wine here ever since — through two relocations, one flood, and the 1931 earthquake, which killed two priests and seven students in this building's chapel.
voice_direction: steady, let the 1931 line land. It's the weight of the story.
fact_check:
- 1851 establishment, French Marists, Pakowhai site. ✅ Mission Estate / Wikipedia.
- Two priests and seven students killed in the stone chapel, 1931 earthquake. ✅ Wikipedia.
- Moved from Pakowhai to Meeanee (1858), then to Greenmeadows (1909) after a flood. Final move — the building was cut into 11 sections and rolled on logs over 5 km in 2 days. ✅ Wikipedia.
- New Zealand's oldest winery. ✅ (Some claim overlap with Babich et al., but 1851 predates them all.)

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
A group of French Marist priests arrived in Hawke's Bay in 1851 with a mission, a lot of faith, and — because they needed sacramental wine and knew from southern France how to make it — a few vine cuttings. They settled first at Pakowhai, near the Ngaruroro River. They planted gardens, kept cattle, and put in vines. That's the founding moment of New Zealand's oldest winery, and also, more or less, the founding moment of the Hawke's Bay wine industry.

It almost didn't survive. In the 1850s, inter-iwi conflict made the original site untenable, and the mission moved to Meeanee in 1858. Then in 1897 came a biblical flood that buried the vineyards in silt. By 1909, they'd decided to move again — this time to higher ground at Greenmeadows, where you are now.

The move is the story everyone tells. The mission building itself — a large wooden seminary — was cut into eleven sections, placed on rollers, and dragged five kilometres uphill by traction engines. It took two days. People rode on top of it.

The building survived the move. Twenty-two years later, on the third of February 1931, it survived the earthquake, too — but not everyone inside did. Two priests and seven seminarians were at prayer in the stone chapel when the quake hit. The chapel collapsed. All nine were killed. There is a memorial on the site.

Today, Mission Estate is still a working winery, with a cellar door, restaurant, and one of the region's grand heritage buildings. The Mission Concert — an annual summer event — is one of the biggest outdoor concerts in New Zealand. But the deepest thing here is the continuity. A hundred and seventy-five years of people, in the same valley, making wine in a tradition that predates almost everything else you can see in this country.
voice_direction: warm, a little reverent. The 1931 paragraph needs space — don't rush the priests and students line. End gently on "continuity."

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The Mōhaka River cuts a dramatic cleft between northern Hawke's Bay and Wairoa — steep, deep, and awkward. In the 1920s, if you wanted to push a railway line up to Gisborne, you had to cross it. No detour was cheaper.

So the Public Works Department designed one of the most ambitious pieces of engineering in New Zealand history. The plan, by engineers John Lelliot Cull and William Langston Newnham, was a steel-truss viaduct ninety-five metres tall and nearly two hundred and seventy-seven metres long — the tallest railway viaduct in the southern hemisphere, then and now.

Construction started in 1930. Then the story gets harder. The Great Depression hit, money ran out, and work was abandoned in 1931 — the same year the Hawke's Bay earthquake tore through the region and damaged the half-finished line. For five years the unfinished piers sat in the gorge.

Work resumed in 1936. When it did, the pace was remarkable: the viaduct was completed in about a year and opened in July 1937. Crossing it by train is still one of the great rail experiences in New Zealand — a long, slow, very high glide above the river. It's a Category 1 Heritage New Zealand listing.

You're most likely seeing it from State Highway 2 at Raupunga, where there's a pull-off with a clear view of the whole structure. The scale isn't obvious until you spot a car on the road below it. Watch for a few minutes. A log train might go over. The steel sings.

A quick note before you drive on: the river below you is the Mōhaka — a taonga river to Ngāti Pāhauwera, who have long fought for its protection. The viaduct is engineering history. The river is older, and more alive.
voice_direction: matter-of-fact on the engineering. Warm on "the steel sings." Respectful on the final paragraph — it's a genuine handoff of frame.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: The hill you're standing on grew in 1931. The earthquake pushed Mataruahou up by about two metres — and drained the lagoon where Napier airport now sits. Everything flat you can see to the north used to be under water.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The hill you're on has a Māori name — Mataruahou — and a job. For centuries it was the pā headland that watched the coast. In 1931 it did something nobody was expecting: it grew.

When the earthquake struck on the third of February, the Pacific Plate and the Australian Plate shifted violently along the fault line that runs under Hawke's Bay. Mataruahou was pushed up by roughly two metres. At the same time, the Ahuriri Lagoon — a vast, shallow inland sea that stretched from right under your feet out to the north — was drained. Tens of square kilometres of seabed dried out in minutes. The Māori fishing villages along the lagoon edge suddenly found themselves high and dry. The lagoon never came back.

From where you're standing, look north. That flat land — the port, the industrial zone, Napier Airport, the suburbs of Onekawa and Pirimai — all of it used to be water. Napier didn't just rebuild itself after the quake; it physically doubled its footprint. The port below expanded, the airport was eventually built on reclaimed land, and a town that had been squeezed onto two small hills suddenly had room to breathe.

The viewpoint is best at sunset or in early morning — the light comes in off the Pacific and the whole bay opens up. On a clear day you can see Mahia Peninsula to the north, Cape Kidnappers to the south, and the long curve of the coastline in between. That curve is the bite of the fish — Te Matau-a-Māui, the fish hook.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: On the third of February, 1931, Napier fell down in two and a half minutes. Two hundred and fifty-six people died. What rose out of the rubble — in thirty months — is the town you're looking at now: the most complete Art Deco seafront in the world.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
On the third of February, 1931, at 10:47 in the morning, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Hawke's Bay. It lasted two and a half minutes. By the time it stopped, Napier had flattened. Two hundred and fifty-six people were dead — most from collapsing chimneys, masonry, and the fires that followed.

The town started rebuilding within weeks. And here's the unlikely part: they chose Art Deco. It was the absolute newest style in the world in 1931 — cinemas in New York had it, ocean liners had it, and now a small New Zealand port town in the middle of the Depression decided it would have it too. The architects worked fast and cheap: reinforced concrete, flat roofs, geometric sunbursts, and a deliberate decision to keep everything low and horizontal — partly as earthquake engineering, partly because the stripped-back, forward-looking lines suited a town that wanted nothing more to do with the Victorian facades that had just killed its citizens.

By 1933 most of the CBD was back. The reason it's still here — and why you can walk along Emerson Street and Tennyson Street and Marine Parade today and see something almost unchanged in ninety years — is that Napier never got rich enough to tear it all down and start again, the way Auckland and Wellington did. What saved it was neglect. Then, in the 1980s, a small group of locals realised what they had and started organising to protect it. Today the Napier Art Deco Trust runs daily walking tours, and the town's annual Art Deco Festival in February is one of the biggest heritage events in the country.

If you only do one thing: walk the block between Emerson and Tennyson after dark, when the neon signs are lit. That's the Napier everyone comes here to see.
voice_direction: warm but not breathless. The facts do the work. Slow down on "two and a half minutes" and "ninety years". The final practical tip should sound like a friend, not a brochure.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: You're about to drive one of New Zealand's most storied pieces of tarseal. Once a Māori track. Then, from 1872, a two-day Cobb & Co coach run. Before World War Two, truck drivers stashed spare gearboxes along the route because the climbs broke them. In 2023, Cyclone Gabrielle closed it for five weeks. Enjoy it. It's earned its reputation.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The road you're about to drive is officially State Highway 5. Locals call it the Napier–Taupō. A hundred and forty-two kilometres, two hours on a good day, four if anything goes wrong — and things go wrong here often.

Start with how old it is. The route began as a Māori inland track, connecting the Heretaunga plains with the vast interior of the central North Island — the Kaingaroa, Lake Taupō, and the volcanic plateau. When the missionary William Colenso walked it in 1845, heading inland, he was following a path that had been used for centuries.

The coaching era started in 1872. On the first of January that year, Cobb & Co launched a Napier-to-Taupō coach service. It was a two-day trip. Horses were changed at Petane — now Eskdale — then up to Te Pōhue for lunch. Then the descent to the Mohaka River, another change at Tarawera, and overnight in a coaching hotel. The road wasn't a road — it was a cut through dense bush, a sequence of saddles and river fords, and a set of hotels that routinely burnt down.

Motorised traffic made things worse before they got better. The two big climbs — Turangakumu and Titiokura — were so hard on trucks that, before the Second World War, transport companies used to stash spare gearboxes at the summits so drivers could swap one out when theirs failed. In the 1950s a new surveying technology called photogrammetry let engineers redesign the alignment; they lowered the Turangakumu summit by a hundred metres. The last long unsealed stretch — the Runanga Deviation — wasn't opened until 1972.

The 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake rerouted the end of the road. It raised the land at Bay View by metres, moved the mouth of the Esk River, and forced a realignment into Napier that's still in place today.

And then — on the 14th of February 2023 — Cyclone Gabrielle. Slips closed the road in multiple places. Marshall's Bridge near Te Pōhue had its supports washed out. The official detour was five hours long: south to Woodville, up the middle of the island, and around. SH5 reopened to daylight-only traffic on the 20th of March, and to full 24-hour use weeks after that.

Every stop you see on this drive — Eskdale, Te Pōhue, Tarawera, the Mohaka Bridge, Opepe — sits on top of that stacked history. Keep your eyes on the road. But when you stop, the stops are worth it.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Long sand, hard surf, and almost nobody. Ocean Beach — Waipuka to the Māori community who have lived here all along — is the rawest coastline in Hawke's Bay. In 1838, William Morris built a whaling station here. In 1887, a ship called the *Go Ahead* broke up offshore. The boiler is still on the rocks.
voice_direction: salty. Don't rush the names. Drop to almost a whisper on "still on the rocks."

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The road over the hill drops you onto one of the most honest beaches in New Zealand. No pier. No promenade. Long golden sand, heavy surf, a scatter of old baches, and the Pacific doing exactly what it wants.

To the small Māori community who've lived here for generations, this is Waipuka. The beach sits between two of the most significant features of the Hawke's Bay coast — Cape Kidnappers to the north, Waimarama and the Motu-o-Kura islets to the south.

In 1838, at the shoulder of the bay, a whaler called William Morris set up the Rangaiika shore station. This was the violent peak of the Pacific whaling economy: Māori crews and European crews working from the beach, boiling blubber, trading muskets and potatoes. The station was short-lived. Most of these coastal operations were gone within fifteen years as the whale populations collapsed.

In 1887, a coastal steamer called the *Go Ahead* came to grief along this stretch. You can still find the iron boiler wedged into the rocks — a hundred and forty years of swell hasn't shifted it. It's one of those artefacts that tells you exactly how much force is moving past here, constantly, most days of the year.

Today, Ocean Beach is surfer country. The break is sand-bottomed, the peaks move around with the weather, and it works in a lot of swells. There are summer surf lifesaving patrols, because the rip off the middle of the beach is no joke — one of the stronger rips on the east coast. Swim between the flags. If there are no flags, walk.

Two rock arches along the southern end at low tide. Nesting shags on the cliffs. If you stand here at dawn with coffee and no one else around, you understand why the people who grew up at Waipuka don't really want it written up too enthusiastically.
voice_direction: respectful of the wildness. The line about locals not wanting it written up too enthusiastically should feel honest — almost an acknowledgement that we're walking a line by featuring it at all.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: You're driving past Opepe. On the 7th of June 1869, nine Bay of Plenty cavalry troopers were killed in a surprise dawn attack here by Te Kooti's forces. One man escaped through the bush barefoot and naked. The graves are still here, signposted off the road.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
You're driving through the site of one of the most consequential ambushes in the New Zealand Wars. It's easy to miss. The Opepe Historic Reserve is signposted off the left, about fifteen kilometres east of Taupō, and it contains nine graves in a small clearing in the bush.

The story begins in early June 1869. Te Kooti Arikirangi — a Rongowhakaata leader, escaped political prisoner, and founder of the Ringatū faith — had been fighting a mobile guerrilla campaign across the central and eastern North Island for nearly two years. Government forces were chasing him. By the first week of June he was in the Taupō region, trying to rally support.

On the 6th of June a detachment of fourteen troopers from the Bay of Plenty Volunteer Cavalry rode into Opepe, an old forest clearing used by travellers. They were tired, they were dismounted, and they made a serious mistake: they took off their boots and their tunics, and set no proper watch.

Just after dawn on the 7th of June, Te Kooti's men came in through the trees. It was over in minutes. Nine of the fourteen troopers were killed. A few escaped into the bush — one, trooper George Crosswell, reportedly ran for kilometres without boots and with almost no clothing, eventually reaching Runanga Station and raising the alarm.

The ambush was a serious military and political shock. It hardened the government campaign against Te Kooti, which chased him through the Urewera until 1872, when he escaped into the King Country and lived there until a pardon in 1883. He continued to lead the Ringatū church — which still has tens of thousands of followers today — until his death in 1893.

The graves at Opepe were maintained by local farmers for decades and are now a Department of Conservation historic reserve, enclosed by a low white fence. A small stand of native bush surrounds them. It takes about twenty minutes to walk the loop. If you stop, you'll find it quiet. It always is.
voice_direction: measured, witness-tone, no dramatisation. Pause briefly on the date. Respect the names. End with stillness.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: On the ninth of October 1769, two worlds met here for the first time. James Cook's Endeavour landed. A Ngāti Oneone rangatira named Te Maro was shot and killed. Cook left two days later, called it Poverty Bay, and the name stuck for two hundred and fifty years. Tūranganui-a-Kiwa is the name that was always here.
voice_direction: grave. No flourishes. The final sentence is the one that matters.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Stand at the mouth of the Tūranganui River and face the sea. This is probably the most historically loaded square kilometre in New Zealand.

The story that gets most of the attention is Cook's. On the seventh of October 1769, a twelve-year-old named Nicholas Young — "Young Nick" — spotted land from the Endeavour's masthead. Two days later, on the ninth, Cook and a small party rowed ashore and became the first Europeans to set foot on New Zealand soil. What followed was not the handshake the Endeavour's journals imagined. Over the first two days, nine Māori were shot and killed. The first was Te Maro, a rangatira of Ngāti Oneone. Cook couldn't get the supplies he'd hoped for, called the bay "Poverty Bay", and sailed south.

But the older story is the one the place has always carried. Centuries earlier — around the 1200s or 1300s — the waka Horouta landed in this same bay, captained by Kīwa. Kīwa settled, and the harbour became Tūranganui-a-Kiwa — "the great standing-place of Kīwa". Another waka captain, Māia, built a pā at Kaiti Beach called Puhi Kai Iti. You're standing on, or very near, its site. The people you can't see — Ngāti Oneone, Rongowhakaata, Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki and others — never left. They've been here twenty generations longer than the story in the plaque.

The way the site is interpreted has changed enormously. For decades the monuments here told the Cook story only. After the 250th anniversary in 2019 — Tuia 250 — that changed. Today at Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site you'll find, alongside the original 1906 Cook obelisk, a set of pou carved by local iwi artists, a re-named reserve, and signage that tells both stories side by side. Up the hill at Titirangi / Kaiti Hill there's a memorial to Te Maro at the Ruatanuika lookout.

Walk here slowly. It's small. But you can feel how much history is pressed into it.
voice_direction: this script is a handoff of frame. Cook's story first (because it's what most listeners come with), then the older and truer context. Slow down on "twenty generations longer." End gently.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Shine Falls is the waterfall most Hawke's Bay locals haven't been to. It's hidden in the Maungaharuru Range, inland from the coast between Napier and Wairoa, inside DOC's Boundary Stream Mainland Island — a reserve where the department has done decades of intense predator control to bring back the forest birds. You're walking into a place that sounds, genuinely, like New Zealand used to sound: tūī, bellbirds, kererū, sometimes a kākā.

The track from Heays Access Road is about an hour and three-quarters one way, through kanuka, kawakawa, kōwhai and tītoki. It's not difficult, but it's remote. There's no reception. There's almost always no one there.

And then, around a corner, the falls. Fifty-eight metres — the tallest in Hawke's Bay — falling clean off a limestone cliff. A "horsetail" waterfall, meaning the water is pinched narrow at the top and fans out over grooves worn in the rock. At the base, a shallow pool with native long-fin eels in it.

A note you need to hear: Cyclone Gabrielle, in February 2023, was catastrophic for this valley. Bridges were destroyed. The track was ripped up in places. DOC has been reopening sections in stages, and conditions change. Check the DOC website before you set out — don't trust an app, including this one, to be up to date on a track this exposed to weather.

And the "mainland island" name, while we're here, is worth understanding. It's a DOC programme where one patch of unfenced forest gets trapped, poisoned, and monitored so intensely that it behaves like a pest-free offshore island. Boundary Stream was one of the original six, set up in the 1990s. You're walking through one of the quiet victories of New Zealand conservation.
voice_direction: intimate, nature-loving. Slow down on the bird list. The Gabrielle paragraph is serious — not performatively so, just clear. End on quiet pride.

It's the Guinness World Record for longest place name in an English-speaking country. And every syllable is telling a single story, about one man, one flute, and a brother lost.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
You're looking at an unremarkable hill. Three hundred and five metres high. Sheep on it. But the sign on the fence at the base is one of the most photographed objects in New Zealand, because it carries this name:

Taumata­whakatangi­hanga­koauau­o­Tamatea­turi­pukaka­piki­maunga­horo­nuku­pokai­whenua­ki­tana­tahu.

Eighty-five letters. The longest place name in any English-speaking country, and by most measures the longest place name in the world. Guinness has officially recognised it since the 1980s.

The name is not random. It's a story, compressed. Tamatea — Tamatea-pōkai-whenua, "Tamatea who encircled the land" — was one of the great early explorers of Aotearoa, whose travels are woven into the whakapapa of iwi up and down the east coast. The name of this hill translates, roughly, as "the summit where Tamatea — the man with the big knees, who climbed mountains, was thrown about by storms, and travelled across the land — played his kōauau to his beloved". In the fuller tradition, Tamatea had been in battle in this area and lost his brother. He stayed, mourning. He played his kōauau — a small bone flute, held between the lips — for his brother, and for the woman he loved. The melody named the hill.

A longer, 105-letter version of the name exists in some recordings, with even more of Tamatea's story packed in. The sign at the road carries the 85-letter version because it's the one that stuck.

The hill itself is on private farmland, so the sign is all most visitors see. That's enough. It's not really a hill. It's a poem, standing on a fence line.

If you're driving the detour down to Pōrangahau from SH2, it's about twenty minutes each way. Pōrangahau itself is a small Ngāti Kere community with a beautiful coastal surf break worth the extra half hour.
voice_direction: the reveal is everything. Slow right down when you read the name itself. Lift gently on "the melody named the hill" — it's the emotional landing. The practical tip at the end should sound friendly, not touristy.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: The ridgeline here is a body. In Ngāti Kahungunu tradition it's the giant Rongokako — lying on his back, head to the north, feet to the south. You're standing on his face.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
The ridgeline here has a shape people notice — long, low, unmistakably human. Head at the northern end, chest rising toward the summit, feet tapering south. In Ngāti Kahungunu tradition this is the body of the giant Rongokako, and the peak itself is his face — Te Mata o Rongokako.

The story goes that Rongokako fell in love with the daughter of Te Whatu-i-āpiti, a chief whose people lived on these plains. To prove his love, he was set a series of tasks — the last of which was to eat his way through the hill. He tried. And here, near the summit, he choked. The ridge you see is where his body fell.

The land was gifted to the people of Hastings in 1927 by the Chambers family, and Te Mata Park has been protected ever since. A mountain-bike park runs the lower slopes; the short summit walk from the car park is about twenty minutes on tarmac, genuinely flat until the last rise. The long walk up from Tauroa Road is ninety minutes through redwood and pine, and worth it.

On a clear day the view opens all the way from the Ruahine Range in the west to Hawke's Bay in the east — the bite of the fish hook. Paragliders launch off the eastern face in late afternoon. Go at dawn if you want the silence. Go at sunset if you want the colour.

A note before you walk up — this is a living ancestor, not a theme park. Please stay on the marked tracks.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Te Pōhue has had three hotels. The first was built for Cobb & Co coach passengers in 1869. The second burnt down in 1897 when a farmer's burn-off threw sparks onto the shingle roof. The third was destroyed by the 1931 earthquake. The current township is what stayed.
voice_direction: dry, matter-of-fact. Each hotel failure gets its own small beat. Quiet on the last line.

- First hotel c.1869. ✅ Wikipedia / Te Ara / HB Knowledge Bank.
- Second hotel burnt 1897, sparks from burn-off. ✅ Wikipedia.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Te Pōhue isn't much — a scattered township around a small lake, about fifty-five kilometres north of Napier, perched on the climb before the Titiokura Saddle. But every square metre of it has a story, because this was where the Cobb & Co coach stopped for lunch.

The first hotel was built here around 1869, just as surveyors were cutting the Napier–Taupō coach road through what was then dense native forest. When the coach service launched on the first of January 1872, Te Pōhue became the mid-morning change-of-horses and feed stop — an essential piece of infrastructure for anyone trying to get from the port at Napier to the interior of the central North Island in a single day.

The hotel had an unlucky run. The first one was replaced, and the second burnt down in 1897 — sparks from a farmer clearing scrub nearby landed on the shingle roof, and the whole thing went up. A third hotel, the Lake Hotel, was built in 1899 by Joseph King on a prime site overlooking the lake. It lasted thirty-two years. Then, at ten forty-seven on the morning of the third of February 1931, the Hawke's Bay earthquake arrived. The Lake Hotel came down.

What you see today is what the township became after that — a rural crossroads with a small school, a fire station, a few homes, and, crucially, a highway servo which has saved a lot of drivers with a dodgy fuel gauge.

The road out of Te Pōhue climbs steeply. In the pre-war truck era, transport companies used to stash spare gearboxes at the summits up ahead because the climbs were famous for wrecking them. When you drop to second gear on the next few corners, you're driving the same hills.
voice_direction: amused and affectionate. This is a small place told as a small place. The gearboxes line should land with a driver's grin.

- Cobb & Co Napier–Taupō service from 1 Jan 1872; Te Pōhue lunch stop. ✅
- Second hotel burnt 1897 from burn-off sparks on shingle roof. ✅
- Lake Hotel built 1899 by Joseph King; destroyed in 1931 quake. ✅
- Gearboxes stashed at summits — corroborating detail from Contractor Magazine heritage piece.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: In the grounds of a tiny school at Te Araroa stands what is, almost certainly, the largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree on earth. Six hundred years old. A branch span of thirty-seven metres. Its name means "the mouth of Rerekohu".
voice_direction: hushed, the way you'd describe a church. Let "thirty-seven metres" land.

- Largest and oldest pōhutukawa in New Zealand — ✅ widely published (Te Ara, iNaturalist, etc.).
- Age: 600+ years. ✅ Estimates cluster around 600, with one study dating it to ~1421 ± 200 years.
- Branch span: over 37 m when measured in 1950. ✅ Te Ara.
- Located at Te Araroa Area School grounds. ✅ Publicly accessible.
- Name "Te Waha o Rerekohu" = "the mouth of Rerekohu" — a historical rangatira of the area. ✅

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
In the grounds of Te Araroa Area School, on the far eastern edge of the North Island, there's a tree that's hard to take in all at once. You have to walk around it, twice, before the size resolves. Pōhutukawa branches don't grow upward — they grow outward and down, making their own shade, making their own forest. This one has a branch span of over thirty-seven metres, the largest ever recorded for the species.

Its name is Te Waha-o-Rerekohu. "The mouth of Rerekohu." Rerekohu was a rangatira of this rohe, and in tradition, kūmara was stored in a pit beneath the tree — the tree was his pātaka, his food store. Its roots are the floor of that store still.

The tree is at least six hundred years old. One study places its germination around the year 1421, which would make it around 600, give or take two centuries. That means it was growing here before Abel Tasman, before Cook, before the British Empire existed, before Te Araroa had its current name. It's older than Christchurch. It might be older than the printing press. It's certainly older than the Dutch Golden Age.

Pōhutukawa is called the New Zealand Christmas tree — for its blood-red flowers in December and January. To see this one in bloom is to see something almost too much for a single photograph: a cliff of red above the school fence.

The tree is on school grounds but is publicly accessible, and visitors are asked to treat it with the care and quiet it deserves. Don't climb. Don't break branches for souvenir. Stand back a few metres and look up.

Just to the north, around the corner, the East Cape Lighthouse watches the first sunrise in the world. Plan to be there at dawn and here by breakfast.
voice_direction: genuinely awed. Drop a beat before "its roots are the floor of that store still." The list of historical markers ("before Tasman, before Cook") should build, not rush. End practical.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: You're at 736 metres, on top of the Maungaharuru Range. East: the whole of Hawke's Bay down to the Pacific. West: the Kaweka Ranges and the Mohaka River. Before the 1950s engineers rebuilt this road, truckies used to burn out gearboxes on the climb up.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Pull over at the top if you can. This is Titiokura Saddle — seven hundred and thirty-six metres up, on the ridgeline of the Maungaharuru Range, and on a clear day it is one of the great understated views on the North Island.

Turn east and you're looking back down the whole of Hawke's Bay — the Heretaunga plains, Napier on the coast, the Pacific beyond. On a summer evening you can watch cloud climb the ranges behind you and pour, slow motion, down toward the sea. Turn the other way and you're facing the inland country: the Kaweka Forest Park, the long folded ridges, and the Mohaka River valley disappearing off toward the volcanic plateau.

This saddle has a harder history than the view suggests. It was part of the original Māori inland track, used for centuries before European arrival. When the missionary William Colenso walked from Napier inland in the 1840s, he climbed this saddle on a footpath. In the 1870s a stockade was built up here as part of the colonial government's chain of outposts along the coaching road, one of which — Opepe, further west — would become the site of a disastrous ambush.

The climb up to where you're standing was so hard on early motor transport that before the Second World War, freight companies stashed spare gearboxes at the summit — genuinely, literally spare gearboxes, sitting under tarps, waiting for the next truck that arrived with a dead one. Major realignments in the 1950s, using the then-new technology of aerial photogrammetry, cut the summit of the next climb west — Turangakumu — by a full hundred metres.

When you drive on, you'll feel the road drop away and then the next big climb lift you up again. It's still an honest piece of road. Keep your eyes up.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Six hundred and sixty metres of reinforced concrete walking out into the Pacific — the longest wharf in the southern hemisphere. It took three years to build in the 1920s. Its last ship called in 1966. It's still there. You can walk every inch of it.
voice_direction: tour-guide factual. Small smile on "you can walk every inch of it."

- 660 m long. ✅ NZ History / Te Ara.
- Longest reinforced-concrete wharf in an open roadstead in the southern hemisphere. ✅
- Construction 1926–1929, designed by Cyrus Williams. ✅

- Last ship Kopara, 1966. ✅ NZ History.
- It's still walkable; restoration trust has maintained it since the 1990s.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Tolaga Bay doesn't have a proper harbour. It has a long, shallow, open beach facing the Pacific. The local name is Ūawa — the estuary. For sheep and wool farmers on this coast in the 1920s, that was a problem. Ships couldn't come in close. Everything had to be barged out to waiting vessels, slowly, expensively, and in weather conditions that were usually trying to kill everyone involved.

So in 1926, marine engineer Cyrus Williams drew up a plan the locals agreed to pay for: a reinforced concrete wharf long enough to reach deep water, out past the swell. The target was 660 metres. Construction started that year. It went wrong almost immediately — storms damaged the works, the approach had to be rerouted, and the harbour board had to borrow another £20,000 to finish. The opening, with the Minister of Marine in attendance, was on 22 November 1929.

At its peak, in 1936, 133 ships a year worked the wharf. Wool out. Supplies and machinery in. Coastal boats like the Kopara were the lifeline of the East Coast before the roads were sealed.

Then the roads got sealed. The coasters stopped calling. In 1966 the Kopara made the last sailing. The wharf became a memory, and started falling to pieces.

What saved it is unusual. In the 1990s a group of locals — the Tolaga Bay Save the Wharf Charitable Trust — decided they couldn't let it go. They've been repairing it section by section for three decades, and it's now Category 1 Heritage New Zealand listed.

Walk out to the end. It takes about eight minutes each way. You can see fish in the water below. On a good day you'll feel the concrete flex a little under the swell — it's designed to. A few hundred metres to the north, the Cooks Cove walkway takes you to where James Cook's Endeavour took shelter in October 1769 and the botanist Joseph Banks first got a good look at New Zealand flora. But the wharf is the main event.
voice_direction: warm, affectionate. This is a story about a small town keeping something alive. The line about concrete flexing under the swell should feel like a shared confidence.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Waipawa was founded in 1860 as "Abbottsford", on a ford across the river. The settlers kept trying. The name Waipawa kept winning. A hundred and sixty years later, it's one of the quietest, most intact colonial main streets in New Zealand — and, long before that, a major Ngāti Kahungunu kainga.
voice_direction: dry, amused by "the settlers kept trying." Let "a hundred and sixty years later" breathe. End with respect.
fact_check:
- Founded 1860 by Frederick Abbott (runholder). ✅ Te Ara + Wikipedia.
- Originally named Abbottsford; Māori name Waipawa preferred by settlers themselves. ✅
- Pre-European: the Waipawa / Tukituki plains were major Ngāti Kahungunu occupation — particularly Ngāi Toroiwaho and Ngāti Whatuiāpiti hapū. ✅ Kahungunu iwi materials.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
Long before it had a main street, this was a crossing. The Waipawa River runs out of the Ruahine Range, and anyone moving north–south on the eastern side of the North Island had to ford it somewhere. Ngāti Kahungunu — specifically the hapū of Ngāi Toroiwaho and Ngāti Whatuiāpiti — had lived on these plains for centuries, with kainga, gardens, and eel weirs up and down the river.

The European chapter starts in 1851. Government agent Donald McLean negotiated the purchase of three huge blocks of Hawke's Bay land — more than 250,000 hectares in total — from Ngāti Kahungunu. That transaction opened Central Hawke's Bay to sheep runholders, and in 1860 a runholder named Frederick Abbott laid out a small town next to the ford. He called it Abbottsford. The other settlers preferred the Māori name. Within a decade the town was Waipawa on every map.

What makes it worth a stop now is what didn't happen. Waipawa never boomed. It was a busy provincial service town from the 1870s to about the 1960s, and then it politely stopped changing. Walk the main street and you'll see wooden shopfronts, a 1920s post office, the old Central Hawke's Bay County Council chambers, and one of the last genuinely in-use small-town pubs in the country. The CHB Settlers Museum, a few minutes away, has the local side of the story — photos, farming gear, the whole pioneering scaffold.

There are two marae in the town — Mataweka and Nohomaiterangi — affiliated with the same hapū who've been here all along. You don't visit them casually. But you do drive past them, and it's worth knowing why they're there.

Practical: coffee at the Red Bridge café, lunch at the Waipawa Hotel, and a quick detour south to the Tukituki River bridge if you like unfashionable New Zealand landscapes.

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text: Pull into the lookout on your right. Waipunga Falls drops forty metres in three tiers straight off the side of SH5 — one of the few major North Island waterfalls you can see without getting out of the car. The river behind it drains the Kaimanawa Ranges.
voice_direction: bright, roadside-friendly. Quick nudge — "on your right" — so drivers know to look now.
fact_check:
- Waipunga Falls ~40 m, three tiers. ✅ DOC / multiple tourism sources.
- Visible directly from SH5 lookout between Te Pōhue and Taupō. ✅

author: Roydon + Claude
narration_text:
There's a lookout coming up on your right in a few hundred metres. Take it. It's one of the easiest stops on this road, and one of the prettiest — Waipunga Falls, a forty-metre, three-tiered drop straight off the edge of the highway.

The river you're looking at is the Waipunga. It rises in the Kaimanawa Range to the west — rough, high country on the edge of the central volcanic plateau — and drains east to join the Mohaka, the big river you crossed further back. The falls themselves sit in a narrow pumice gorge. The top tier is the tallest; below it the water steps through two lower drops before disappearing into bush. In winter after heavy rain the whole thing thunders. In summer it narrows to a clean white ribbon.

What makes this stop unusual is that you don't have to walk anywhere. The lookout is literally built for drivers — a short barrier at the edge of the highway, a view across the valley straight onto the falls, and enough room to stretch your legs for a minute. In a country where most of the big waterfalls demand a tramp, this one gives itself up for free.

The bush you're looking across is the remains of a much bigger native forest that once covered everything from here up to Lake Taupō. The survey parties cutting the Napier–Taupō coach road in the 1870s described forest so dense they could only make out the sky directly above the track. Most of it is gone now, cleared for pasture a century ago. This gorge is part of what's left.

If you're travelling with kids, this is the toilet-stop-and-a-photo combination the drive needs. If you're on your own, stand a minute. Look. It'll still be falling when you go.
